coredata.dc:title: A novel red emission carbon quantum dots-based aptasensor for detection of potassium ions in vitro and in vivo and its application in plant imaging
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yanhua
Zhang, Xuan
Zhang, Quanxi
Guo, Donggang
Xu, Qiang
Wang, Huimin
coredata.dc:description: The determination of potassium ion (K+) concentration in organisms is of particular importance. Herein, an efficient, sensitive, and eco-friendly fluorescence aptasensor for detecting potassium ions (K+) was designed based on the interaction of aptamer (K-Apt), complementary sequence of aptamer (C-Apt), red emission carbon quantum dots (R-CDs), and graphene oxide (GO). Red luminescent CDs modified with K+ aptamer(R-CDs@K-Apt) and C-Apt modified GO (C-Apt@GO) were used as the fluorescence donor and acceptor, respectively, in this aptasensor. K+ was detected sensitively using the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) method. The linear range of this aptasensor was 5.0 × 10−9–2.2 × 10−7 mol/L, and its detection limit was as low as 6.2 × 10−11 mol/L. Importantly, this aptasensor can be employed for quantitative K+ detection in serum because of the enhancement of its selectivity and biocompatibility by the modification of biomolecules including K-Apt and C-Apt. Furthermore, the aptasensor’s red emission at 625 nm enabled it to completely avoid the background fluorescence caused by chlorophyll in wheat seedlings, allowing it to display the K+ concentration of the wheat through plant imaging. These findings lay a foundation for the development of a novel detection technique for analyzing the distribution of K+ in the body fluids of animals and plants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Red emission carbon quantum dots
Aptasensor
Potassium ion
Detection
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spectral characterization of R-CDs and R-CDs@K-Apt
Spectral characterization of GO and C-Apt@GO
Optimization of the experimental conditions parameters
The aptasensor selectivity and interference resistance detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The obtained TEM image (Inset: particle size distribution of R-CDs), as well as the (B) fluorescence excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra for the prepared R-CDs.
(A) The acquired FT-IR and (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra for the prepared R-CDs and R-CDs@K-Apt.

(A)The acquired fluorescence emission spectra for the developed aptasensor at selected concentrations of K+ (5.0 × 10−9, 1.0 × 10−8, 3.0 × 10−8, 6.0 × 10−8, 9.0 × 10−8, 1.2 × 10−7, 1.5 × 10−7, 1.8 × 10−7, 2.1 × 10−7, 2.2 × 10−7 mol/L); (B) linear relationship of this aptasensor at various K+ concentrations.
Fluorescent imaging of K+ aptasensor with K+ in live wheat. (A) Control (B) and experimental group of wheat roots (C), Experimental group of wheat stems (D) and leaves.
Sensing mechanism employed by the proposed aptasensor for the detection of K+.
Analytical results of K+ in serum samples by the K+ aptasensor and ICP-MS.
Comparison of the developed method with previously published techniques used for K+ detection based on aptamers.

head.ce:title: A novel red emission carbon quantum dots-based aptasensor for detection of potassium ions in vitro and in vivo and its application in plant imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Resource, Shanxi Laboratory for Yellow River, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment and Resource, Shanxi Laboratory for Yellow River, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi University of Electronic Science and Technology, Linfen 041000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi University of Electronic Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi University of Electronic Science and Technology, Linfen 041000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030000, PR China (Y. He).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The determination of potassium ion (K+) concentration in organisms is of particular importance. Herein, an efficient, sensitive, and eco-friendly fluorescence aptasensor for detecting potassium ions (K+) was designed based on the interaction of aptamer (K-Apt), complementary sequence of aptamer (C-Apt), red emission carbon quantum dots (R-CDs), and graphene oxide (GO). Red luminescent CDs modified with K+ aptamer(R-CDs@K-Apt) and C-Apt modified GO (C-Apt@GO) were used as the fluorescence donor and acceptor, respectively, in this aptasensor. K+ was detected sensitively using the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) method. The linear range of this aptasensor was 5.0 × 10−9–2.2 × 10−7 mol/L, and its detection limit was as low as 6.2 × 10−11 mol/L. Importantly, this aptasensor can be employed for quantitative K+ detection in serum because of the enhancement of its selectivity and biocompatibility by the modification of biomolecules including K-Apt and C-Apt. Furthermore, the aptasensor’s red emission at 625 nm enabled it to completely avoid the background fluorescence caused by chlorophyll in wheat seedlings, allowing it to display the K+ concentration of the wheat through plant imaging. These findings lay a foundation for the development of a novel detection technique for analyzing the distribution of K+ in the body fluids of animals and plants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Potassium ion (K+) is an essential nutrient for organisms and has a significant role to play in preserving the normal physiological functions of animals and plants [1]. For instance, it can maintain the osmotic pressure of animal cells, control hormone release, cell apoptosis, blood pressure, pH level, and the concentration of other ions within cells [2,3]. Appropriate supplementation of K+ can protect the structural integrity of plant cell membranes, promote the efficiency of photosynthesis in crops, regulate the active functioning of antioxidant enzymes such as Peroxidase (POD), superoxide dismutase (SOD), and catalase (CAT), and enhance the defense capabilities of antioxidant systems [4,5]. Excessive use of K+ can hinder the absorption of essential cations such as calcium (Ca2+) and magnesium (Mg2+) by plants. This can result in decreased plant productivity and potentially impact the K+ levels in animals and humans through the food chain. Consequently, it can disrupt the normal functioning of vital organs such as the kidneys, heart, nervous system, and cardiovascular system, and even lead to death [5,6]. Hence, the development of highly sensitive and efficient detection methods for K+ both in vitro and in vivo is extremely important to achieve plant imaging. This would allow for a better understanding of the physiological and pathological mechanisms associated with K+ and facilitate the monitoring of K+-related diseases in animals and plants.

Aptamer is a type of biological recognition element that exhibits a high affinity for specific target molecules, hence enhancing the sensitivity of the detection system [6,7]. In 2012, Ma et al. [8] utilized the in vitro SELEX system (systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment) to identify a particular aptamer sequence (K-Apt) for K+. The sequence was determined to be 5′-GGT TGG TGT GGT TGG-3′. Experimental evidence demonstrated that this aptamer can form a stable tetrameric structure upon binding to K+, thereby preventing interference from other ions. Electrochemistry [9], ion chromatography [10], photochemistry [11], and numerous otherswere subsequently developed by researchers todetect K+ using aptamer as the recognition element. Among them, the fluorescence aptamer sensor detection method is preferred because of its high sensitivity, efficiency, ease of use, convenience, rapid speed, and excellent biocompatibility [11,12].

Current fluorescence detection methods that utilize aptamers as recognition elements employ fluorescent probes, such as organic fluorophores [12], fluorescein (FAM) [13], and quantum dots [14]. These probes have spectral ranges primarily in the ultraviolet/visible light region. However, they are susceptible to interference from chlorophyll background fluorescence and scattered light. Furthermore, the toxic effects of organic fluorescent probes restrict their application in attaining effective in vivo detection and imaging. Red-emitting carbon quantum dots (R-CDs) have been found to possess favorable biocompatibility, exceptional photostability, lack of light flickering, and minimal photobleaching. These properties enable R-CDs to effectively avoid noise interference from endogenous chemicals in the body [14]. Therefore, they are often combined with appropriate fluorescent quenching agents to prepare fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensors for efficient biological detection.

Graphene oxide (GO) is a commonly employed detection method due to its large specific surface area, excellent water dispersibility, broad absorption spectrum range, and abundant carboxyl groups on its surface [15]. These carboxyl groups can be modified to enhance the selectivity and biocompatibility of the quencher [16,17]. However, most of the previous studies have accomplished FRETand detected targets by using the π–π stacking interactions between GOand single-stranded DNA/apt. However, this approach has its limitations in terms of selectivity and hydrophobic interactions. According to reports, the sensitivity of detection can be enhanced by coupling apt and GO as FRET receptors [18,19].

In light of the crucial need for K+ detection and the benefits associated with nucleic acid aptamers, R-CDs, GO, and FRET strategies, K+ aptamers (K-Apt) were coupled to the surface of R-CDs to generate (R-CDs@K-Apt) as a fluorescence donor. The complementary sequence of aptamers (C-Apt) on the surface of GO was modified to generate (C-Apt@GO) as a fluorescence acceptor, to prepare a specific aptasensor (CNs@K-Apt/C-Apt@GO) for K+. The presence of a hydrogen bond between C-Apt and K-Apt causes a reduction in the distance measured between C-Apt@GO and R-CDs@K-Apt. This leads to FRET, resulting in reduction of fluorescence within the prepared system of R-CDs@K-Apt/C-Apt@GO. However, the addition of K+ to the detection system causes the K-Apt to fold into a G tetrad structure upon binding to K+. As a result, the K-Apt loses its binding site for C-Apt, leading to a gradual disappearance of FRET. The degree of fluorescence recovery is proportional to the quantity of K+ introduced, allowing for the detection of K+ through the prepared system of R-CDs@K-Apt/K+/C-Apt@GO.

The GO employed in this investigation was produced using the modified Hummers approach [20], as described in the Supporting information 1.2. The R-CDs were developed via a previously described one-step hydrothermal process [21]. In brief, citric acid (1.5 g) and neutral red (2.1 mg) with a molar ratio of 1000:1 were added to double-distilled water (5 mL) and agitated for 2 min to produce a homogenous mixed solution. The prepared mixture was thereafter moved to an autoclave (25 mL) made of stainless-steel, where it was placed for 4 h at 180 °C to produce a purple R-CDs solution. After centrifuging the solution for 10 min at 10,000 rpm to eliminate any deposits of black solid, the supernatant was filtered again using a microporous membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm. Finally, the R-CDs powder was prepared via freeze-drying.

To couple K-Apt to the R-CDs surface, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide-N-hydroxysuccinimide ester was employed as a coupling agent to facilitate the reaction between the COOH group on the R-CDs surface and the NH2 group at the 5′-end of K-Apt, forming an amide bond. The R-CDs solution (1 mL, 1 mg/mL) was prepared using buffer saline sodium citrate (pH 7.8) as the solvent. Fresh solutions of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) (1 mL, 20 mM) and N-hydroxysuccinimide ester (1 mL, 200 mM) were prepared and their mixture was allowed to react for 3 h. Then, K-Apt (43.5 µg, 1.3 OD) was poured into the mixture and stirred for 24 h at ambient conditions. The resultant solution was centrifuged three times at 8000 rpm for 20 min using an ultrafiltration tube (3KD). Finally, the eluent was obtained by eluting with saline sodium citrate and it is referred to as R-CDs@K-Apt.

The synthesis of the C-Apt and GO coupling was carried out utilizing the C-Apt excess technique, following an improved method described in the literature [20,21]. In an ice bath, fresh solutions of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) (1 mL, 15 mM) and N-hydroxysuccinimide ester (1 mL, 30 mM) to the GO dispersion (0.5 mg/mL) were poured. The reaction was then allowed to continue for 4 h to ensure that the COOH on the GO surface was completely activated. After adding 49.5 µg (1.5 OD) of C-Apt modified with NH2, the pH value of the resultant solution was then adjusted to 7.8 through the buffer solution saline sodium citrate. The reaction then took place overnight at ambient temperature. The mixture underwent centrifugation and was washed three times to eliminate any excess C-Apt. The resulting precipitate was collected to get the C-Apt-conjugated GO (C-Apt@GO). Consequently, in the following experiments, the concentration of C-Apt@GO was represented by the concentration of GO.

The K+ detection reaction comprised two stages: fluorescence quenching resulting from the FRET reaction, and fluorescence recovery caused by the removal of FRET. The reaction was conducted in a 10 mL colorimetric tube. First, R-CDs@K-Apt (14.5 μg/mL, 1 mL) and 30 μL of C-Apt@GO with 0.5 mg/mL concentration were poured into a colorimetric tube, and the volume was adjusted to 5 mL using saline sodium citrate. Initially, R-CDs@K-Apt (1 mL, 14.5 μg/mL) and C-Apt@GO (30 μL, 0.5 mg/mL) were introduced into the colorimetric tube. The volume was then increased to 5 mL using saline sodium citrate, where the mixture was raised to a temperature of 37 °C for about 20 min. The fluorescence intensity was measured at ambient temperature. Subsequently, different concentrations of K+ were introduced into the R-CDs@K-Apt/C-Apt@GO system, where the reaction took place at ambient conditions for 15 min. The fluorescence emission was studied at 625 nm via a spectrophotometer, using a 530 nm excitation source. The emission and excitation slit widths were kept at a value of 10 nm. To reduce the influence of background interference, a relative signal output value of the fluorescence was taken that was represented as F/F 0 − 1, Where F0 and F denote fluorescence intensities of our aptamers without and with K+, respectively.

The experimental material used was “Pinyu 8012” wheat. The seeds were disinfected by immersing them in a 10 % NaClO solution for 30 min. After thorough rinsing, the seeds were soaked in sterile ultrapure water for 8 h. They were then wrapped in a wet piece of gauze and placed under dark conditions at 37 °C for 24 h. The germinated wheat seeds were soaked in moist perlite in a dark surrounding at 30 °C. Once the wheat seeds developed cotyledons, the plant growth chamber's dark conditions changed with a light intensity of 2000 lx. Additionally, the light–dark cycle was adjusted to simulate the natural environment, consisting of 16 h of light at a constant temperature of 28 °C, followed by 8 h of darkness at 18 °C.

When C-Apt@GO was introduced to the R-CDs@K-Apt solution, K-Apt and C-Apt combined to produce double-stranded DNA, bringing the R-CDs@K-Apt and C-Apt@GO closer together (Scheme 1 ). Furthermore, the emission spectrum of R-CDs@K-Apt extensively overlapped with the absorption spectrum of C-Apt@GO, leading to FRET [24,25]. Comparing curves a and b in Fig. S1, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the detection system decreased by 71.6 % after C-Apt@GO (3 µg/mL) was added into to the system of R-CDs@K-Apt.

However, upon adding K+ to the prepared R-CDs@K-Apt/C-Apt@GO system, R-CDs@K-Apt detached from C-Apt@GO and selectively bound to K+, forming intermolecular G-quadruplex structures [26,27]. This led to the disappearance of FRET, and also the fluorescence of the R-CDs@K-Apt/K+/C-Apt@GO system gradually recovered (Fig. S1 curve c). The recovery rate isrelated directly to the number of K+ added, leading to an accurate detection of the quantitative K+.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed for the morphological analysis of the R-CDs. As presented in the inset of Fig. 1 (A), R-CDs are uniformly dispersed and roughly 3.5 nm in size. Fig. 1(B) shows that the greatest excitation peak for R-CDs is at 530 nm (curve a). The emission spectrum (curve b) ranges between 610 and 640 nm, showing that R-CDs were successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characterization of R-CDs and R-CDs@K-Apt
ce:section.ce:para: R-CDs were effectively prepared by doping nitrogen from neutral red into the carbon of citric acid, as demonstrated by the existence of the CN bond found at 1396 cm−1 within the infrared spectrum of the prepared R-CDs [19] (Fig. 2 A). Furthermore, the peaks found at 3438, 1727, 1551, and 1217 cm−1 corresponded to the OH, CO, CC, and COC stretching vibrations, respectively. This showed that the produced CDs included a significant number of carboxyl groups, have good water solubility, and can serve as binding sites for K-Apt amino group coupling [22]. As compared to R-CDs, R-CDs@K-Apt displayed the stretching vibration of CN at 1405 and 1092 cm−1, NH at 2976 cm−1, NCO at 1648 cm−1, and NCH at 1568 cm−1 [23]. UV absorption spectroscopy experiments were also performed, as illustrated in Fig. 2B. A new and distinctive aptamer absorption peak was observed at 260 nm for R-CDs@K-Apt in comparison to the UV image of R-CDs. The above analysis suggests a strong coupling of K-Apt with R-CDs, thus allowing the successful preparation of the complex R-CDs@K-Apt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characterization of GO and C-Apt@GO
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. S2, it is evident that GO displayed stretching vibration peaks for OH at 3413 cm−1, CO at 1398 and 1052 cm−1, along with COH at 1620 and 1225 cm−1. Consistent with earlier studies, the FTIR spectrum of GO shows that GO rich in COOH was successfully prepared [21]. Upon interaction with C-Apt, the complex displayed distinctive peaks associated with NH, NCO, NCH, and CN bonds at 2989, 1648, 1574, and 1478 cm−1 [23]. This observation suggests the successful coupling of the COOH from GO with the NH2 from C-Apt, resulting in the formation of the composite quencher C-Apt@GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions parameters
ce:section.ce:para: Various factors could influence the performance of the designed K+ aptasensor. They comprise the K-Apt and C-Apt concentrations during the coupling stage and the R-CDs@K-Apt and C-Apt@GO concentrations during the detection stage. Furthermore, the time of the fluorescence recovery and quenching phases (time a and b) can affect the overall detection result. As a result, these parameters were optimized beforeK+ detection. To improve the selectivity of the FRET sensor, the coupling reaction must have enough amount of K-Apt and C-Apt sequences. It is crucial to avoid an excess of K-Apt and C-Apt, as this may result in self-entanglement, potentially impacting subsequent experiments. The concentrations of K-Apt and C-Apt were first optimized, as illustrated in Fig. S3(A, B). The optimal concentrations for K-Apt and C-Apt coupling were determined to be 14.5 and 16.5 µg/mL, respectively.

The F/F0 − 1 value increased gradually as R-CDs@K-Apt concentration increased, as illustrated in Fig. S3C, reaching a peak at 2.9 µg/mL. Subsequent increases in concentration resulted in self-absorption of R-CDs@K-Apt, contributing to elevated background interference [28,29]. Hence, the optimal R-CDs@K-Apt concentration was determined to be 2.9 µg/mL. On this basis, we optimized the concentration of C-Apt@GO. In Fig. S3D, when the concentration of C-Apt@GO increased, more R-CDs@K-Apt were gradually bound to C-Apt@GO, eventually reaching a peak at 3 µg/mL. The recovery of the fluorescence signal became more difficult as the C-Apt@GO concentration increased [30]. Thus, the concentration of C-Apt@GO was established at 3 µg/mL.

Additionally, the impact of incubation temperature on the binding between R-CDs@K-Apt and C-Apt@GO was examined, as depicted in Fig. S3E. At 37 °C, the highest biological activity and binding rate of K-Apt and C-Apt were observed. Thus, the optimal temperature for the fluorescence quenching stage was determined to be 37 °C. Employing the same method, the optimal time for the fluorescence reduction stage (time a) and the fluorescence recovery stage (time b) was found to be 20 and 15 min, respectively (Fig. S3F).

The detection efficacy of the K+ adaptable sensor using R-CDs and GO was evaluated after the reaction conditions were optimized. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, with increasing K+ concentration, the fluorescence signal of the detection system exhibited a progressive increase at 625 nm. A good linear correlation was observed between K+ concentrations ranging from 5.0 × 10−9–2.2 × 10−7 mol/L: F/F0 − 1 = 0.0054C(K+) + 0.0812, having a correlation coefficient (R2) value of 0.994 (refer to Fig. 3B). The detection limit of the adaptable sensor was determined to be 6.2 × 10−11 mol/L using the formula LOD = 3σ/S, where σ is used to express the standard deviation of the blank solution fluorescence value and S denotes the gradient of the obtained calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The aptasensor selectivity and interference resistance detection
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment was carried out using 5.0 × 10−8 mol/L K+ or the 10-fold concentration of other ions (Na+, Ca2+, Li+, Mg2+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ag+, and Al3+) that are frequently found in biological fluids, and the change in fluorescence intensity was observed under the same experimental parameters. As depicted in Fig. S4, the fluorescence of the K+-containing system increased dramatically upon comparison with the blank group, while the fluorescence intensity of the other ion-related systems showed only slight changes (<5%). The observed findings demonstrated the good selectivity of the developed method toward K+ detection.

The practical applicability of the aptasensor for K+ detection was determined by measuring the recovery rate of spiked K+ in serum samples. Serum samples were 100-fold diluted and spiked with various K+ concentrations (1.0 × 10−8, 5.0 × 10−8, 1.0 × 10−7 mol/L), and the fluorescence intensity change was assessed using the established approach. The spiking recovery rate was obtained using the standard curve, as illustrated in Table 1 . The three groups of samples exhibited spiking recovery rates that ranged from 96.52 %–102.56 %. Meanwhile, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) method was further used to determine the concentration of K+ in serum samples, with the recovery rate ranging from 97.2 %–104.3 % (Table 1). This result is consistent with the established K+ aptasensor method. The experimental results indicated the applicability of the developed sensor to be used for detecting K+ in real samples.

Considering the importance of normal K+ concentration for plant growth, further investigation was carried out regarding the application of K+ aptasensor in wheat seedlings through plant imaging. Initially, the toxicity of K+ aptasensor was investigated against HeLa cells via standard MTT assay.

Following the incubation of HeLa cells with varying concentrations of the K+ aptasensor (0, 2.9, 14.5, 29, 58, 87 µg/mL, denoted by the concentration of R-CDs@K-Apt) for 24 h at 37 °C, it was observed that the survival rate of HeLa cells consistently exceeded 90 % (Fig. S5), indicating the low cytotoxicity of K+ aptasensor which is capable to be used for biological imaging.

Based on this fact, the R-CDs@K-Apt/C-Apt@GO aptasensor was investigated for its ability to image K+ in living plants. Following a one-hour treatment of wheat seedlings with the K+ aptasensor, the experimental group was then added with K+ (2.0 × 10−8 mol/L) and subjected for a further 30 min of incubation followed by washing of the residual probe with ultra-pure water three times. After that, the samples were analyzed using laser confocal microscopy (Fig. 4 A) and no significant fluorescence signal was observed in the control group (without the addition of K+). Conversely, the experimental group exhibited bright red fluorescence in the roots, stems, and leaves as depicted in Fig. 4B, C, and D, respectively.

These findings substantiate the potential applicability of the K+ aptasensor for in vivo imaging of K+ in living plants, thereby underscoring its practical utility and relevance in plant biology research.

Furthermore, in comparison to recently published techniques for K+ detection, while employing aptamers as recognition elements (Table 2 ), the developed demonstrates sensitivity either comparable to or surpassing other methods, emphasizing its efficacy in K+ detection [9,11,31–33]. Excluding the fluorescent assay based on blue carbon quantum dots [34] or green dye [35] as a fluorescent probe and electrochemistry [26]. However, compared to electrochemical methods, this technique eliminates the necessity for complex pre-treatment during the detection process. The developed R-CDs were found to be more environmentally friendly than green dyes. Moreover, they demonstrated superior penetration capabilities into plant tissues in comparison to blue-emitting carbon quantum dots. This attribute helps in mitigating the interference from spontaneous fluorescence and scattered light emitted by organisms, ultimately leading to improved imaging effects. Consequently, these advantages extend the spectrum of applications for plant-related studies. Therefore, the observed findings suggest that the developed method can be efficiently employed for both in vitro and in vivo detection of K+.

In summary, a new FRET-based aptasensor was developed and achieved efficient, sensitive, and environmentally friendly detection of K+. The advantages observed in this study can be attributed to the simple and rapid process of nanomaterials (R-CDs and GO) preparation; The modification of biomolecules (K-Apt and C-Apt) significantly enhanced the specificity and biocompatibility of aptasensor; The affinity of K-Apt for both K+ and C-Apt, coupled with its stronger affinity for K+ compared to C-Apt, facilitates the occurrence and disappearance of FRET, thereby substantially improving the reaction rate. The major limitation of this method is that the detection limit of this method is not the lowest of all K+ detection methods, but it has low toxicity and superior optical properties, it presents a viable approach for detecting K+ levels in wheat seedlings. This not only introduces novel perspectives for applying the method in plant physiology research but also offers innovative strategies for leveraging nanobiotechnology in the realms of health monitoring and growth regulation in both animals and plants.

Yanhua He: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Xuan Zhang: Writing – original draft. Quanxi Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Donggang Guo: Conceptualization, Supervision. Qiang Xu: Writing – review & editing. Huimin Wang: Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work were completed with the support of the Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths, China (202203021212390), Science and Technology Innovation Project of Colleges in Shanxi Province (2022L623), and the Philosophy and Social Sciences Research Project of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi Province (2021zsszsx180).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111478.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Metabolites
